Author: Serge van den Oever
Title: GitHub Copilot — tip 1 — the agents have arrived!
Text:
GitHub Copilot — tip 1 — the agents have arrived!
We hear all those stories about GitHub Copilot being obsolete, because we now have new kids on the block like Cursor, Windsurf, Cline and many more. If YouTube knows about your interest in AI, LLM and development IDE’s, you are bombarded with videos on why GitHub Copilot is dead, and Cursor and Windsurf are our future, or the other way around, depending on the time of day and phase of the moon. All these new AI tools utilize the Visual Studio Code platform either by creating a unique fork to be able to get the deep integrations required for their product, or by providing an extension.
Cursor
I must say I was impressed by the features of Cursor. Especially in the “Composer” mode I did not have to provide the files to act on, and it was able to build the required context for prompting the underlying LLM by itself. But as a heavy user of Visual Studio Code for many years I did not like the changes Cursor made to the standard Visual Studio Code. I understand they want to create a unique feeling to their version of the product, but I am the kind of guy that always orders the same dish in a restaurant because I know it tastes good, and that taste I was thinking about when I booked that restaurant. So, for me the Visual Studio Code layout is good enough!
Soon in my use of Cursor I was out of free tokens, and had to decide: go for the pro mode, or back to GitHub Copilot:
Cline
Cline takes a different approach; it integrates into standard Visual Studio Code. And the cool thing with Cline, you can bring your own models — either running locally, or an API key for a service in the cloud, like Azure OpenAI.
My standard question when I try out an LLM with code is to build me a Space Invaders game. Cline did. But on one question it kept iterating, and I was burning through my pay-as-you-go API tokens so quickly that after a million tokens I quickly stopped using it.
GitHub Copilot
From my company we have access to GitHub Copilot for Business, which gives a feeling of confidence regarding your privacy and the privacy of your code, as Microsoft will not connect to third parties to run their models, but will run the underlying models in their own datacenters (although I was surprised to learn that it uses AWS instead of Azure), and on their own model instances:
It is a kind or rat-race between the different AI infused IDE’s, all storming towards the agentic era. I understand the GitHub Copilot team can’t move as fast as these smaller companies, because they have a huge installed base, can’t afford mistakes, and have to protect a reputation.
Where I had a feeling of being “left behind” using GitHub Copilot, with just a better code-completion and a nice chatting window, I was really happy to see the work the GitHub Copilot team has done with their new release which is currently only available in the Visual Studio Code Insiders version (just another version of Visual Studio Code which you can run side-by-side with the “normal” Visual Studio Code), where they made it possible to toggle between Edit and Agent mode in the Copilot Edits. And it is really good! The “agent in action” screenshot below gives a great indication what is happening:
I asked the agent to refactor two header strings to constants, because I had an uppercase/lowercase issue with how I passed the header in the frontend versus how it was received in my API endpoint.
In the right Copilot Edits pane, you can see the conversation of the agent:
- It understands the question
- It searches the codebase for the relevant files/context to work on
- It creates new files, or amends files where necessary
- It validates the changes made to the files, also looking at the output in the compile window (I hope) to see what goes wrong
- Comes with an “end report” of all the things that were done
Although you can directly accept the changes, I prefer to go through the changed files to see what is exactly done:
You can either go through the file one by one in inline view, I really can’t make any chocolate of what I am seeing. But that is because I am color-blind to a certain extent. No clue what the colors mean in this screen:
I personally prefer the diff-editor view, which you can select on each changed file:
This switches the view to the diff view I normally use to check my merge conflicts:
Another cool feature if the “View all edit” option:
This option provides an overview of all the changes on all files in one view:
Trusting the agent
Can we always trust the agent to do the good thing? No. I had a few times that the agent did not understand me correctly. It produced good-looking code. I approved it, without looking at the changes it made. Then I concluded it messed with my UI, moved buttons around, and introduced a button for an action that was part of my hamburger menu. That was a wise lesson. I now first read the agent’s interpretation of the assignment and always check the diff view of the changes it made.
Conclusion
I am impressed. This is such a productivity boost. And I am happy I can stick with my good old Visual Studio Code which has been a great companion for almost 10 years, and hopefully for the next 10 years to come. Of course, I look at all the other AI coding tools on the market, I try them out, see what they are capable of, and assess if they can help me in my daily job. For example, https://v0.dev from Vercel is a tool I really like, and that I use for both my professional job and my personal hobby projects. But I am glad that the GitHub Copilot team keeps up with the other players and exposes the most powerful models available within their product, under the same license fee.
